text: During the total laparoscopic hysterectomy, the surgeon navigates around the fatty bowels of the obese patient, frequently lifting them to maintain a clear view of the operative field.

The surgeon cuts the left round ligament and continues dissection lateral to the eye pivot, maneuvering to relieve the bowel from the operative field. The surgeon frequently moves the hand to pull up the bowel, creating a window below the IPv.

The surgeon skeletonizes the vessel by removing the peritoneum, following the Clermont-Ferrand technique to create a window below the infundibulopelvic ligament. The vessel is sealed over a three-centimeter length, allowing safe cutting of the infundibulopelvic ligament.
The assistants pull the uterus towards the sacrum while the surgeon retracts the bladder to identify and develop the dissection plane between the bladder and the vagina.
The surgeon continues dissection toward the right round ligament, carefully identifying the correct dissecting plane to avoid potential bleeding.

The surgeon controls the right round ligament and continues dissection toward the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament. The right ureter is visible, with the bowel closely positioned, requiring frequent manual retraction to maintain a clear operative field. The surgeon begins skeletonizing the right round ligament, highlighting the challenge of managing bowel proximity in obese patients.

The surgeon maintains a pneumoperitoneum of ten millimeters due to patient obesity. The posterior leaf of the round ligament is cut, and the right uterine pedicle is controlled by sealing the vessel without cutting to manage backflow. The uterine artery is sealed over three centimeters but remains uncut. The focus shifts to controlling the left uterine pedicle.

The surgeon cuts the posterior leaf of the brown ligament, carefully skeletonizing the vessels in the process. The dissection progresses toward the upper part of the left uterus sacrum, reaching the posterior valve of the ulterior manipulator.

The surgeon performs vesico-vascular dissection, cutting both medial aspects of the enlarged bladder. A lateral dissection is conducted to lower the bladder without tension. The left uterine artery is cut, and dissection continues medial to the uterine artery to control the cervical vaginal vessels. The bowel is visible near the operative field.

The surgeon cuts tissue medial to the uterine artery, ensuring no ureteral damage. The uterine artery is controlled, and a stitch is placed through the bladder peritoneum to elevate the bladder, improving the view of the vagina. A monocular hook in coagulating mode is used for further dissection.

The surgeon cuts the lateral and posterior aspects of the vagina for improved hemostasis, facilitating the extraction of a small uterus. The bladder pexy is visible, aiding in the procedure as the assistant pulls up the bowel, allowing the surgeon to close the vagina independently.

The surgeon uses an autoblock count water knot to begin closing the lateral vaginal aspect. The needle is passed through the entire mucosa and the residual utero-sacral ligament to decrease the risk of vaginal prolapse.

The surgeon uses an interactive suture technique for vaginal closure, with the assistant's hand visible pulling up the bowel to maintain a clear surgical field. The depth of the surgical site is evident, and intracorporeal knotting is employed for precise closure.
